Based on an interaction graph, a subclass of methods employs graph neural networks (GNNs) to learn the communication, enabling agents to improve their internal representations by enriching them with information exchanged. With growing research, we note a lack of explicit structure and framework to distinguish and classify MARL approaches with communication based on GNNs. Thus, this paper surveys recent works in this field. We propose a generalized GNN-based communication process with the goal of making the underlying concepts behind the methods more obvious and accessible.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys MARL methods using GNNs for communication and proposes a generalized framework to clarify concepts in this domain.
AI.motivation: Modern MARL methods increasingly rely on communication mechanisms for efficient coordination. GNNs provide a robust way to integrate these communications, but existing work lacks clear classifications and understanding.
AI.method: The authors surveyed recent literature and proposed a generalized communication process for MARL methods that use GNNs, aiming to simplify and classify these approaches.
AI.result: A structured framework for GNN-based communication in MARL was introduced, helping to describe and categorize existing methodologies.
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances accessibility and understanding of MARL communication approaches using GNNs, benefiting future research in the domain.

title: Evaluating the Alignment Between GeoAI Explanations and Domain Knowledge in Satellite-Based Flood Mapping
summary: The increasing number of satellites has improved the temporal resolution of Earth observation, making satellite-based flood mapping a promising approach for operational flood monitoring. Deep learning-based approaches for flood mapping using satellite imagery, an important application within Geospatial Artificial Intelligence (GeoAI), have shown improved predictive performance by learning complex spatial and spectral patterns from large volumes of remote sensing data. However, the opaque decision-making processes of deep learning models remain a major barrier to their integration into critical scientific and operational workflows. This highlights the need for a systematic assessment of whether model explanations align with established domain knowledge in remote sensing. To address this research gap, this study introduces the ADAGE (Alignment between Domain Knowledge And GeoAI Explanation Evaluation) framework. The proposed framework is designed to systematically evaluate how well explanations of deep learning models align with established remote sensing knowledge, particularly regarding the distinctive spectral properties of the Earth's surface. The ADAGE framework employs Channel-Group SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) method to estimate the contributions of grouped input channels to pixel-level predictions. Experiments on two satellite-based flood mapping tasks demonstrate that the ADAGE framework can (1) quantitatively assess the alignment between model explanations and reference explanations derived from domain knowledge and (2) help domain experts identify misaligned explanations through alignment scores. This study contributes to bridging the gap between explainability and domain knowledge in GeoAI for Earth observation, enhancing the applicability of GeoAI models in scientific and operational workflows.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the ADAGE framework to evaluate how deep learning model explanations align with remote sensing domain knowledge for flood mapping using satellite data.
AI.motivation: The opaque decision-making processes of deep learning models limit their integration into critical scientific and operational workflows, necessitating mechanisms to align model explanations with established domain knowledge.
AI.method: The paper employs the Channel-Group SHAP method within the ADAGE framework to evaluate model explanations against the spectral properties of Earth's surface derived from remote sensing knowledge.
AI.result: ADAGE quantitatively assesses explanation alignment and provides alignment scores, aiding experts in identifying misaligned explanations across two satellite flood mapping tasks.
AI.conclusion: This study advances GeoAI explainability by bridging gaps between model explanations and domain knowledge, promoting their usability in operational Earth observation workflows.

title: Causal Learning with Neural Assemblies
summary: Can Neural Assemblies -- groups of neurons that fire together and strengthen through co-activation -- learn the direction of causal influence between variables? While established as a computationally general substrate for classification, parsing, and planning, neural assemblies have not yet been shown to internalize causal directionality. We demonstrate that the inherent operations of neural assemblies -- projection, local plasticity control, and sparse winner selection -- are sufficient for directional learning. We introduce DIRECT (DIRectional Edge Coupling/Training), a mechanism that co-activates source and target assemblies under an adaptive gain schedule to internalize directed relations. Unlike backpropagation-based methods, DIRECT relies solely on local plasticity, making the resulting causal claims auditable at the mechanism level. Our findings are verified through a dual-readout validation strategy: (i) synaptic-strength asymmetry, measuring the emergent weight gap between forward and reverse links, and (ii) functional propagation overlap, quantifying the reliability of directional signal flow. Across multiple domains, the framework achieves perfect structural recovery under a supervised, known-structure setting. These results establish neural assemblies as an auditable bridge between biologically plausible dynamics and formal causal models, offering an "explainable by design" framework where causal claims are traceable to specific neural winners and synaptic asymmetries.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that neural assemblies can internalize causal directionality using a new mechanism called DIRECT, and validates their effectiveness through structural recovery tests.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to investigate whether neural assemblies, known for computational tasks, can be utilized to understand causal directionality between variables.
AI.method: The proposed mechanism, DIRECT, co-activates source and target assemblies using adaptive gain schedules. Validation is conducted through synaptic-strength asymmetry and functional propagation overlap.
AI.result: Neural assemblies with DIRECT demonstrate perfect structural recovery in a supervised setting across various domains.
AI.conclusion: Neural assemblies serve as a biologically plausible and auditable model capable of tracing causal claims to specific neural configurations and dynamics.

title: Exploring the Efficiency of 3D-Stacked AI Chip Architecture for LLM Inference with Voxel
summary:
To overcome the well-known memory bottleneck of AI chips, 3D stacked architectures that employ advanced packaging technology with high-density through-silicon vias (TSVs) pins have proven to be a promising solution. The 3D-stacked AI chip enables ultra-high memory bandwidth between compute and memory by stacking numerous DRAM banks atop many AI cores in a distributed manner. However, it is not easy to explore the efficiency of the 3D-stacked AI chip, due to its unique distributed nature. And we need to carefully consider multiple intertwined factors that range from upper-level computing paradigm to machine learning (ML) compiler optimizations, and to the underlying hardware architecture.
  In this paper, we develop Voxel, a fast and compiler-aware end-to-end simulation framework to facilitate exploring the efficiency of 3D-stacked AI chips for large language model (LLM) inference. Voxel enables the software/hardware co-exploration by employing a programming interface that allows ML compilers to customize the model execution plans. After validating the results of Voxel with an emulator on real silicon, we thoroughly examine the impact and correlation of different aspects of 3D-stacked AI chips, including state-of-the-art compute paradigms, tile-to-core mapping, tensor-to-bank mapping, NoC topologies and link bandwidth, DRAM bank bandwidth, per-core SRAM capacity, and energy/thermal constraints. Our findings disclose that the end-to-end efficiency of a 3D stacked AI chip not only is determined by the cooperative function of these factors, but also significantly depends on the mappings from tiles to AI core and DRAM banks. We report our findings throughout the paper, with the expectation that they will shed light on the development of the 3D-stacked AI chip ecosystem. We will open source Voxel and our study results for public research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Voxel, a simulation framework for efficiently exploring 3D-stacked AI chip design for LLM inference and highlights key interconnected factors influencing chip performance.
AI.motivation: Addressing the memory bottleneck in AI chips through 3D-stacked architectures, while overcoming challenges in evaluating their efficiency due to their distributed nature.
AI.method: The development of Voxel, an end-to-end simulation framework that enables software/hardware co-exploration for 3D-stacked AI chips, validated by an emulator on real hardware.
AI.result: Voxel facilitates deep analysis of interconnected factors, revealing that mappings of tiles to cores and DRAM significantly impact end-to-end efficiency, alongside compute paradigms and hardware constraints.
AI.conclusion: Voxel provides valuable insights into the co-exploration of 3D-stacked AI chips, contributing tools and findings expected to inform ecosystem development; the framework and results will be open-sourced.

title: FloatSOM: GPU-Accelerated, Distributed, Topology-Flexible Self-Organizing Maps
summary: GPU-accelerated Self-Organizing Map (SOM) implementations are among the most competitive options for large-scale SOM analysis, but growing dataset sizes increasingly challenge their practical use because workloads no longer fit cleanly within device-memory limits. We introduce FloatSOM, a SOM framework for scalable training and deployment that supports multi-GPU execution, out-of-memory disk-backed streaming, and novel topologies beyond regular lattices. We evaluate FloatSOM on 14 synthetic and real benchmark datasets together with controlled speed scaling benchmarks, and show that these improved topologies, combined with topology-aware hyperparameter fine-tuning, yield lower quantization error than current state-of-the-art SOM baselines. FloatSOM also sustains this performance at large scale with high-throughput distributed execution; in the largest benchmark, it trains a 1024-node SOM network on 1,000,000,000 samples with 50 features in 6.16 minutes on 8 GPUs across two separate high-performance-computing nodes.

AI.tldr: FloatSOM is a new GPU-accelerated SOM framework that enables scalable and efficient training with support for multi-GPU execution, disk-backed streaming, and novel topologies, enabling superior quantization error performance and high efficiency on large datasets.
AI.motivation: To address memory constraints and inefficiency in current GPU-accelerated SOM implementations, especially for very large datasets that exceed device memory limits.
AI.method: Development of FloatSOM, introducing scalable features like multi-GPU support, disk-backed streaming, and expanded topologies, coupled with topology-aware hyperparameter tuning. Performance was benchmarked against synthetic and real datasets.
AI.result: FloatSOM outperformed state-of-the-art SOM baselines in terms of quantization error. In a distributed execution setup, it processed a 1024-node SOM on a billion samples with 50 features in just 6.16 minutes using 8 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: FloatSOM effectively addresses scaling challenges in SOM implementations, achieving both superior performance and scalability for very large-scale datasets with advanced computational methods.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Persona Induction from User Behavioral Logs: Learning Evidence-Grounded and Truthful Personas
summary: Behavioral logs provide rich signals for user modeling, but are noisy and interleaved across diverse intents. Recent work uses LLMs to generate interpretable natural-language personas from user logs, yet evaluation often emphasizes downstream utility, providing limited assurance of persona quality itself. We propose a hierarchical framework that aggregates user actions into intent memories and induces multiple evidence-grounded personas by clustering and labeling these memories. We formulate persona induction as an optimization problem over persona quality-captured by cluster cohesion, persona-evidence alignment, and persona truthfulness-and train the persona model using a groupwise extension of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Experiments on a large-scale service log and two public datasets show that our method induces more coherent, evidence-grounded, and trustworthy personas, while also improving future interaction prediction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a hierarchical framework that transforms user actions into interpretable personas by clustering intent memories and optimizing persona quality metrics.
AI.motivation: The need to improve the quality and interpretability of personas generated from noisy and diverse user logs to enhance user modeling.
AI.method: The authors employ clustering and labeling of intent memories within a hierarchical framework, coupled with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), to optimize capturing persona quality based on specific metrics.
AI.result: Using both large-scale service log data and public datasets, the methodology generates personas that are more coherent, evidence-grounded, and trustworthy, while improving interaction predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively enhances persona quality and utility, demonstrating its potential to improve user modeling processes.

title: ImproBR: Bug Report Improver Using LLMs
summary: Bug tracking systems play a crucial role in software maintenance, yet developers frequently struggle with low-quality user-submitted reports that omit essential details such as Steps to Reproduce (S2R), Observed Behavior (OB), and Expected Behavior (EB). We propose ImproBR, an LLM-based pipeline that automatically detects and improves bug reports by addressing missing, incomplete, and ambiguous S2R, OB, and EB sections. ImproBR employs a hybrid detector combining fine-tuned DistilBERT, heuristic analysis, and an LLM analyzer, guided by GPT-4o mini with section-specific few-shot prompts and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline grounded in Minecraft Wiki domain knowledge. Evaluated on Mojira, ImproBR improved structural completeness from 7.9% to 96.4%, more than doubled the proportion of executable S2R from 28.8% to 67.6%, and raised fully reproducible bug reports from 1 to 13 across 139 challenging real-world reports.

AI.tldr: They developed ImproBR, an LLM-based pipeline to enhance low-quality bug reports, significantly improving reproducibility and completeness in reports from Mojira.
AI.motivation: Developers face challenges with low-quality user-submitted bug reports that often lack crucial details like Steps to Reproduce (S2R), Observed Behavior (OB), and Expected Behavior (EB).
AI.method: ImproBR, a hybrid automated detection and enhancement pipeline, leverages fine-tuned DistilBERT, heuristic tools, and an LLM analyzer, supported by GPT-4o mini and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline.
AI.result: ImproBR enhanced structural completeness from 7.9% to 96.4%, executable S2R proportions from 28.8% to 67.6%, and increased fully reproducible reports from 1 to 13 in a real-world dataset of 139 bug reports.
AI.conclusion: ImproBR effectively improves the completeness, clarity, and reproducibility of bug reports, offering a practical solution for improving software maintenance processes.

title: FlowS: One-Step Motion Prediction via Local Transport Conditioning
summary: Generative motion prediction must satisfy three simultaneous requirements for real-world autonomy: high accuracy, diverse multimodal futures, and strictly bounded latency. Diffusion models meet the first two but violate the third, requiring tens to hundreds of denoising steps. We identify a conditioning strategy that resolves this tension: \textit{single-step integration is accurate when the underlying transport problem is local}. A model that must both discover the correct behavioral mode and traverse a long displacement in one step accumulates large discretization errors; conditioning the base distribution to lie near plausible futures reduces the problem to short-range refinement, the regime where a single Euler step suffices. We instantiate this \emph{local transport conditioning} in FlowS, a conditional flow matching framework with two mechanisms. First, an online, scene-conditioned learned prior emits $K$ calibrated anchor trajectories per agent, each already near a plausible future, converting mode discovery into local correction. Second, a step-consistent displacement field enforces semigroup self-consistency, guaranteeing that a single step inherits multi-step accuracy. Crucially, anchoring this field at learned priors along straight-line paths yields a {stable, low-variance} training target, unlike prior self-consistency methods that suffer from {high-variance bootstrap} signals on curved diffusion paths. On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, FlowS achieves state-of-the-art Soft mAP {(0.4804) and mAP (0.4703) with ensemble at 75\,FPS} with single-step inference, demonstrating that local transport conditioning makes one-step generative motion prediction practical for safety-critical autonomy. Code and pretrained models will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new framework called FlowS for generative motion prediction that combines accuracy, multimodal diversity, and real-time capability. It utilizes a novel conditioning technique to achieve these goals in a single step.
AI.motivation: Generative motion prediction models need to be both accurate and diverse, while still being low-latency for real-time applications. Current diffusion models provide accuracy and diversity but fail in terms of latency, which limits their usability in time-critical systems.
AI.method: The authors propose 'local transport conditioning,' a strategy that ensures accurate single-step predictions by constraining the model's base distribution near plausible future scenarios. The FlowS framework includes two key mechanisms: (1) a scene-conditioned learned prior that generates $K$ anchor trajectories for initial localization, and (2) a step-consistent displacement field that guarantees accuracy and stability in single-step predictions, anchored along straight-line paths.
AI.result: FlowS achieves state-of-the-art performance on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, showing Soft mAP of 0.4804 and mAP of 0.4703 with ensemble predictions at an inference speed of 75 FPS.
AI.conclusion: The proposed FlowS framework successfully bridges the gap between accuracy, diversity, and real-time capabilities in generative motion prediction. Its novel local transport conditioning strategy is key to its practicality and strong performance in safety-critical scenarios.

title: Evaluation Revisited: A Taxonomy of Evaluation Concerns in Natural Language Processing
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted a growing body of work that questions the methodology of prevailing evaluation practices. However, many such critiques have already been extensively debated in natural language processing (NLP): a field with a long history of methodological reflection on evaluation. We conduct a scoping review of research on evaluation concerns in NLP and develop a taxonomy, synthesizing recurring positions and trade-offs within each area. We also discuss practical implications of the taxonomy, including a structured checklist to support more deliberate evaluation design and interpretation. By situating contemporary debates within their historical context, this work provides a consolidated reference for reasoning about evaluation practices.

AI.tldr: The paper examines and categorizes recurring evaluation concerns in NLP, offering a taxonomy and checklist for improved evaluation methodology.
AI.motivation: To address persistent methodological debates in evaluating large language models (LLMs) by building on the historical perspectives in NLP.
AI.method: Conducted a scoping review of prior research on NLP evaluation concerns, developed a taxonomy, and proposed a structured checklist.
AI.result: A synthesized taxonomy highlighting recurring issues and trade-offs in NLP evaluation and a checklist to aid evaluation practices.
AI.conclusion: The study contributes a consolidated framework for understanding and improving evaluation methodologies in NLP, grounding contemporary debates in historical context.

title: Probabilistic data quality assessment for structural monitoring data via outlier-resistant conditional diffusion model
summary: Data quality assessment is an essential step that ensures the reliability of the subsequent structural health monitoring (SHM) tasks. This study proposes a prediction deviation-based SHM data quality assessment method using a univariate implicit auto-regressive model, enabling outlier diagnosis and data cleaning. The proposed conditional diffusion model (CDM) augments the standard diffusion model with a conditional embedding module to incorporate temporal context, quartile normalization to mitigate distribution skew, and a Huber loss to enhance robustness against outliers. Within this univariate implicit autoregressive framework, each data point is assigned an outlier probability, quantifying its degree of "outlier-ness", and a global quality evaluation score is computed to characterize the overall dataset quality. Extensive case studies utilizing operational data from real-world structures demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves the accuracy of data quality assessment, outperforming other strong baselines representative of clustering, isolation-based, and deep reconstruction methods. The effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework are further demonstrated by the findings of ablation experiments and hyperparameter analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new SHM data quality assessment method using a predictive, univariate auto-regressive model and a conditional diffusion model (CDM), showing superior effectiveness in identifying outliers and evaluating dataset quality.
AI.motivation: To address the critical need for reliable structural health monitoring (SHM) tasks through enhanced data quality assessment.
AI.method: The method employs a conditional diffusion model (CDM) with innovations such as temporal context embedding, quartile normalization, and Huber loss. Outlier probabilities are assigned to each data point, and a global quality score is calculated within a univariate implicit autoregressive framework.
AI.result: Extensive case studies confirmed that the proposed method improves data quality assessment accuracy, outperforming baseline methods like clustering and isolation-based techniques. Ablation experiments and hyperparameter analysis further validated its robustness.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the efficacy of the proposed CDM-based method in enhancing SHM data quality assessment, making it a valuable tool for real-world applications.

title: Rethinking KV Cache Eviction via a Unified Information-Theoretic Objective
summary: Key-value (KV) caching is essential for large language model inference, yet its memory overhead poses a critical bottleneck for long-context generation. Existing eviction policies predominantly rely on empirical heuristics, lacking a rigorous theoretical foundation. This work rethinks KV cache eviction through the lens of the Information Bottleneck principle. Under a linear-Gaussian surrogate of attention, we derive a closed-form mutual information objective that characterizes the effective information capacity of a retained KV cache subset. This formulation reveals that a wide range of existing eviction strategies can be interpreted as different approximations of the same capacity-maximization principle. Guided by this insight, we introduce CapKV, a capacity-aware eviction method that directly targets information preservation via a log-determinant approximation using statistical leverage scores. This approach replaces heuristic selection with a theoretically grounded mechanism that preserves the maximum predictive signal. Extensive experiments across multiple models and long-context benchmarks show that CapKV consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving a better trade-off between memory efficiency and generational fidelity.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CapKV, a theoretically grounded key-value cache eviction method, which improves memory efficiency and generational fidelity for long-context generation in language models.
AI.motivation: Existing KV caching eviction policies rely on heuristics and lack a theoretical foundation, limiting their efficacy in managing memory overhead during large-language-model inference.
AI.method: The authors reframe KV cache eviction using the Information Bottleneck principle, deriving a mutual information objective for cache retention and introducing CapKV. This method uses statistical leverage scores to approximate information preservation directly.
AI.result: CapKV demonstrates superior performance across various models and long-context benchmarks by achieving an improved balance between memory usage and output quality.
AI.conclusion: CapKV provides a theoretically-backed alternative to heuristic-based eviction strategies, enhancing both memory efficiency and prediction accuracy in long-context language model inference.

title: RADIO-ViPE: Online Tightly Coupled Multi-Modal Fusion for Open-Vocabulary Semantic SLAM in Dynamic Environments
summary: We present RADIO-ViPE (Reduce All Domains Into One -- Video Pose Engine), an online semantic SLAM system that enables geometry-aware open-vocabulary grounding, associating arbitrary natural language queries with localized 3D regions and objects in dynamic environments. Unlike existing approaches that require calibrated, posed RGB-D input, RADIO-ViPE operates directly on raw monocular RGB video streams, requiring no prior camera intrinsics, depth sensors, or pose initialization. The system tightly couples multi-modal embeddings -- spanning vision and language -- derived from agglomerative foundation models (e.g., RADIO) with geometric scene information. This coupling takes place in initialization, optimization and factor graph connections to improve the consistency of the map from multiple modalities. The optimization is wrapped within adaptive robust kernels, designed to handle both actively moving objects and agent-displaced scene elements (e.g., furniture rearranged during ego-centric session). Experiments demonstrate that RADIO-ViPE achieves state-of-the-art results on the dynamic TUM-RGBD benchmark while maintaining competitive performance against offline open-vocabulary methods that rely on calibrated data and static scene assumptions. RADIO-ViPE bridges a critical gap in real-world deployment, enabling robust open-vocabulary semantic grounding for autonomous robotics and unconstrained in-the-wild video streams. Project page: https://be2rlab.github.io/radio_vipe

AI.tldr: RADIO-ViPE is a semantic SLAM system allowing open-vocabulary grounding of 3D objects in dynamic environments using monocular RGB video, without requiring camera calibration or depth sensors.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of current semantic SLAM systems that need calibrated setups and cannot handle dynamic and open environments effectively.
AI.method: RADIO-ViPE couples multi-modal embeddings from foundation models with geometric scene data at various processing stages, utilizing adaptive robust kernels to account for scene and object dynamics.
AI.result: The system achieves state-of-the-art results on the dynamic TUM-RGBD benchmark and matches offline open-vocabulary methods under static assumptions.
AI.conclusion: RADIO-ViPE facilitates real-world deployment of semantic grounding, enabling open-vocabulary 3D understanding in dynamic and unconstrained environments.

title: No Tile Left Behind: Multiprogramming for Surface-Code Architectures
summary: Fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) is emerging as the architectural regime in which practical large-scale quantum workloads will execute. In this setting, however, multiprogramming is no longer a matter of partitioning a flat pool of qubits. Quantum error correction exposes a structured floorplan of data tiles, ancilla tiles, and magic-state service resources, so concurrent execution must account for compact placement, connectivity, routing headroom, and shared support infrastructure. This makes FTQC multiprogramming fundamentally harder than its NISQ counterpart: admission decisions can fragment the remaining floorplan, conservative reservations can waste ancilla, and dynamic contention across data, ancilla, and magic-state resources can degrade both throughput and quality of service. In this work, we develop a formal framework for FTQC multiprogramming that captures these structural constraints and their runtime implications. We formulate the baseline static allocation problem, extend it to limited-resource and online settings through hierarchy-aware scheduling policies, and further generalize it to cultivation-enabled architectures with dynamic magic-state generation. Through simulation on synthetic Clifford+T workloads, the proposed scheduler achieves a normalized system speedup of 3.1x, improving over prior FTQC multiprogramming baselines by ~29% while maintaining low mean slowdown.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenges of multiprogramming in fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) environments by developing a formal scheduling framework that improves system performance significantly and efficiently.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to manage the complex resource constraints in FTQC multiprogramming, a regime where structured resource floorplans and dynamic contention complicate execution and degrade performance.
AI.method: The authors propose a formal framework for FTQC multiprogramming, addressing resource constraints through static allocation, hierarchy-aware scheduling policies, and dynamic magic-state generation in cultivation-enabled architectures.
AI.result: The proposed scheduler demonstrates a 3.1x normalized system speedup and improves performance by ~29% over existing FTQC multiprogramming baselines, while ensuring low mean slowdown in synthetic workload simulations.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively mitigates FTQC multiprogramming challenges, optimizing throughput and quality of service, and represents a significant advancement in scheduling for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

title: OMEGA: Optimizing Machine Learning by Evaluating Generated Algorithms
comment: ICLR 2026: Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement
summary: In order to automate AI research we introduce a full, end-to-end framework, OMEGA: Optimizing Machine learning by Evaluating Generated Algorithms, that starts at idea generation and ends with executable code. Our system combines structured meta-prompt engineering with executable code generation to create new ML classifiers. The OMEGA framework has been utilized to generate several novel algorithms that outperform scikit-learn baselines across a robust selection of 20 benchmark datasets (infinity-bench). You can access models discussed in this paper and more in the python package: pip install omega-models.

AI.tldr: The OMEGA framework automates AI research by generating and evaluating new ML algorithms, outperforming scikit-learn across 20 benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to automate the end-to-end process of AI research and algorithm generation to improve efficiency and innovation in machine learning.
AI.method: The framework combines structured meta-prompt engineering with executable code generation to create novel machine learning classifiers.
AI.result: OMEGA successfully produced ML algorithms that surpass scikit-learn baselines on 20 benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: OMEGA demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of automating AI algorithm generation, opening pathways for further research and application in the field.

title: AI Observability for Large Language Model Systems: A Multi-Layer Analysis of Monitoring Approaches from Confidence Calibration to Infrastructure Tracing
summary: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) in production environments has created an urgent need for observability systems that span the full stack -- from model internals to GPU kernels. Yet existing monitoring approaches address isolated layers of this stack, and no comprehensive analysis has examined how these techniques relate, overlap, or complement each other. This paper presents a structured analysis of five recent research contributions (2025-2026) that collectively define the emerging landscape of AI observability: confidence calibration via reinforcement learning (MIT), internal state monitoring through propositional probes (UC Berkeley), chain-of-thought monitorability evaluation (OpenAI), autonomous cloud operations benchmarking (Microsoft Research, UC Berkeley, UIUC), and non-intrusive inference-level tracing (TRUFFLD). We organize these contributions into a five-layer observability taxonomy, synthesize their key findings into a unified comparison, and identify four critical gaps that remain unaddressed. We further contextualize these research directions against practical operational observability systems that translate infrastructure telemetry into actionable insights for site reliability teams. Our analysis reveals that while individual monitoring layers have matured rapidly, the integration challenge -- connecting model-level confidence signals with infrastructure-level anomalies into coherent operational intelligence -- remains the defining open problem for the field.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes five key research contributions (2025-2026) in AI observability, categorizing them, synthesizing findings, and highlighting unaddressed gaps, particularly the challenge of integrating model-level and infrastructure-level observability.
AI.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) in production requires comprehensive observability systems, but no current monitoring solution spans the entire AI stack, creating an urgent need for unified approaches.
AI.method: The authors provide a structured analysis of five cutting-edge research contributions (confidence calibration, internal state monitoring, monitorability evaluation, cloud operations benchmarking, and inference-level tracing), organizing them into layers within an observability taxonomy and conducting a unified comparison.
AI.result: The paper synthesizes findings into a five-layer taxonomy, outlines their individual advancements, and identifies four critical gaps, emphasizing a major integration challenge in linking model and infrastructure observability.
AI.conclusion: While significant progress has been made in individual observability layers, the field lacks unified systems that bridge model intelligence with infrastructure anomalies, marking the integration challenge as a pivotal open problem.

title: Lights Out: A Nighttime UAV Localization Framework Using Thermal Imagery and Semantic 3D Maps
summary: Reliable backup localization for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) operating in GNSS-denied nighttime conditions remains an open challenge due to the severe modality gap between daytime RGB maps and nighttime thermal imagery. This work presents a semantic reprojection framework for map-relative nighttime UAV localization by aligning segmented thermal observations with a globally referenced, semantically labeled 3D map constructed from daytime RGB data. Rather than relying on appearance-based correspondence, localization is formulated in a shared semantic domain and solved via a symmetric bidirectional reprojection objective with confusion-aware weighting to improve robustness under segmentation uncertainty. The approach is evaluated offline across 6.5 km of nighttime, real-world UAV flight trajectories in urban and semi-structured environments. Relative to RTK GNSS ground truth, the system achieves a bias-corrected RMSE2D of 2.18 m and a median RMSE2D of 1.52 m. Results show that localization performance is strongly correlated with the availability of semantic edge evidence and that large-error events are spatially localized to semantically ambiguous areas rather than uniformly distributed. These findings indicate that semantic reprojection offers a promising pathway toward globally referenced nighttime UAV localization using thermal imagery alone.

AI.tldr: This paper tackles the challenge of nighttime UAV localization in GNSS-denied conditions using thermal imagery, by introducing a semantic reprojection framework leveraging daytime RGB-derived 3D semantic maps.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the difficulty of accurately localizing UAVs at night using their thermal imagery due to the substantial modality gap between daytime optical data and nighttime thermal observations.
AI.method: The authors propose a semantic reprojection framework that aligns segmented nighttime thermal images with globally referenced 3D maps created from daytime RGB data, utilizing a symmetric bidirectional reprojection objective with confusion-aware weighting.
AI.result: Their evaluation across 6.5 km of real-world nighttime UAV flights demonstrates high localization accuracy, achieving a bias-corrected RMSE2D of 2.18 m and median RMSE2D of 1.52 m, with errors concentrated in semantically ambiguous areas.
AI.conclusion: Semantic reprojection provides an effective technique for globally referenced nighttime UAV localization using thermal imagery, offering robustness in environments with segmentation uncertainty.

title: Generative AI-Based Virtual Assistant using Retrieval-Augmented Generation: An evaluation study for bachelor projects
comment: Accepted at BNAIC/BeNeLearn 2024, to appear in Springer CCIS series. 15 pages + refs. Code and survey available at https://github.com/DikaVer/maastricht_university_generative_virtual_assistant
summary: Large Language Models have been increasingly employed in the creation of Virtual Assistants due to their ability to generate human-like text and handle complex inquiries. While these models hold great promise, challenges such as hallucinations, missing information, and the difficulty of providing accurate and context-specific responses persist, particularly when applied to highly specialized content domains. In this paper, we focus on addressing these challenges by developing a virtual assistant designed to support students at Maastricht University in navigating project-specific regulations. We propose a virtual assistant based on a Retrieval-Augmented Generation system that enhances the accuracy and reliability of responses by integrating up-to-date, domain-specific knowledge. Through a robust evaluation framework and real-life testing, we demonstrate that our virtual assistant can effectively meet the needs of students while addressing the inherent challenges of applying Large Language Models to a specialized educational context. This work contributes to the ongoing discourse on improving LLM-based systems for specific applications and highlights areas for further research.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a Retrieval-Augmented Generation-based virtual assistant to enhance the response accuracy of Large Language Models, aimed at helping students with project-specific regulations.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models, despite their capabilities, face difficulties like hallucinations and context inaccuracies when handling specialized domains such as education regulations.
AI.method: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation system was implemented to integrate accurate, domain-specific knowledge into a virtual assistant for students.
AI.result: The virtual assistant effectively met student needs and addressed challenges in specialized educational contexts through evaluation and real-life testing.
AI.conclusion: This research provides insights into improving LLM-based systems for specialized applications and identifies areas for future study.

title: Deep-testing: the case of dependence detection
summary: Deep learning methods have proved highly effective for classification and image recognition problems. In this paper, we ask whether this success can be transferred to hypothesis testing: if a neural network can distinguish, for example, an image of a handwritten digit from another, can it also distinguish an "image of a sample" (such as a scatter plot) generated under a given statistical model from one generated outside that model? Motivated by this idea, we propose a novel procedure called deep-testing, which approaches the classical inferential problem of hypothesis testing through deep learning. More specifically, the test statistic is a classification map learned by a deep neural network from simulated data satisfying the null and alternative hypotheses, leveraging its strong discriminating power to construct a highly powerful test. As a proof of concept, we apply deep-testing to the problem of independence testing, arguably one of the most important problems in statistics. In a large-scale simulation study, deep-testing achieves the highest overall power against nineteen competing methods across a broad range of complex dependence structures, confirming the viability of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a deep-learning-based methodology called deep-testing for statistical hypothesis testing, achieving high performance in independence testing compared to other methods.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether deep learning's success in classification tasks can be applied to hypothesis testing in statistical models.
AI.method: Proposes the deep-testing procedure, employing a neural network to learn a classification map from simulated null and alternative data, leveraging its ability to discriminate between statistical conditions.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance in independence testing, outperforming nineteen competing methods across a diverse set of dependence structures.
AI.conclusion: Validates deep-testing as a powerful and viable approach to hypothesis testing through deep learning.

title: Mini-Batch Class Composition Bias in Link Prediction
comment: Accepted at GCLR 2026: the 5th Workshop on Graphs and more Complex Structures For Learning and Reasoning, colocated with AAAI 2026
summary: Prior work on node classification has shown that Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) can learn representations that transfer across graphs, when underlying graph properties are shared. For a fixed graph, one would then expect GNNs trained for link prediction to learn a representation consistent with that learnt for node classification. We show this intuition does not hold in the general case. Instead, we find popular link prediction models can learn a trivial mini-batch dependent heuristic, enabled by batch-normalisation layers, to solve the edge classification task. When correcting for this, we observe increased alignment of the network representation with node-class relevant features, suggesting the network has learnt a graph representation that better aligns with the underlying graph's properties. Our findings suggest that standard link prediction training may be leading us to overestimate link predictors' ability to learn a generalised representation of a graph that is consistent across tasks.

AI.tldr: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) used for link prediction often fail to align their representations with node classification tasks due to reliance on mini-batch heuristics, potentially limiting generalization.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether GNNs trained on link prediction learn representations that align with or generalize to node classification tasks.
AI.method: Analyzed how batch-normalization layers in link prediction models create trivial mini-batch dependent heuristics and evaluated representation alignment correction.
AI.result: Discovered that popular link prediction models rely on trivial heuristics and correcting this increases alignment with node-class features.
AI.conclusion: Standard link prediction training may lead to overestimations of GNNs' ability to generalize representations across graph-related tasks.

title: FruitProM-V2: Robust Probabilistic Maturity Estimation and Detection of Fruits and Vegetables
summary: Accurate fruit maturity identification is essential for determining harvest timing, as incorrect assessment directly affects yield and post-harvest quality. Although ripening is a continuous biological process, vision-based maturity estimation is typically formulated as a multi-class classification task, which imposes sharp boundaries between visually similar stages. To examine this limitation, we perform an annotation reliability study with two independent annotators on a held-out tomato dataset and observe disagreement concentrated near adjacent maturity stages. Motivated by this observation, we model maturity as a latent continuous variable and predict it probabilistically using a distributional detection head, converting the distribution into class probabilities through the cumulative distribution function (CDF). The proposed formulation maintains comparable performance to a standard detector under clean labels while better representing uncertainty. Furthermore, when controlled label noise is introduced during training, the probabilistic model demonstrates improved robustness relative to the baseline, indicating that explicitly modeling maturity uncertainty leads to more reliable visual maturity estimation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method that treats fruit maturity as a continuous attribute rather than discrete classifications, improving accuracy and robustness to label noise.
AI.motivation: Current vision-based fruit maturity detection approaches use discrete classifications, which impose artificial boundaries and may cause inaccuracies, particularly in visually similar stages.
AI.method: The researchers model fruit maturity as a latent continuous variable and utilize a probabilistic detection head, converting distributions into class probabilities using the cumulative distribution function (CDF).
AI.result: The proposed method shows comparable performance with standard classification models under clean labels and demonstrates improved robustness when label noise is introduced.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling fruit maturity uncertainty as a continuous variable results in a more reliable estimation method, especially under noisy data conditions.

title: MPI Malleability Validation under Replayed Real-World HPC Conditions
summary: Dynamic Resource Management (DRM) techniques can be leveraged to maximize throughput and resource utilization in computational clusters. Although DRM has been extensively studied through analytical workloads and simulations, skepticism persists among end administrators and users regarding their feasibility under real-world conditions. To address this problem, we propose a novel methodology for validating DRM techniques, such as malleability, in realistic scenarios that reproduce actual cluster conditions of jobs and users by replaying workload logs on a High-performance Computing (HPC) infrastructure. Our methodology is capable of adapting the workload to the target cluster. We evaluate our methodology in a malleability-enabled 125-node partition of the Marenostrum 5 supercomputer. Our results validate the proposed method and assess the benefits of MPI malleability on a novel use case of a pioneer user of malleability (our "PhD Student"): parallel efficiency-aware malleability reduced a malleable workload time by 27% without delaying the baseline workload, although introducing queueing delays for individual jobs, but maintaining the resource utilization rate.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a methodology to validate Dynamic Resource Management (DRM) techniques, like malleability, using workload logs replayed on real HPC infrastructures. Results show effective workload optimization without sacrificing overall resource utilization.
AI.motivation: Address skepticism among administrators and users regarding the practical feasibility of DRM techniques in real-world HPC environments.
AI.method: Workload logs are replayed on a 125-node partition of a malleability-enabled Marenostrum 5 HPC cluster to simulate realistic system conditions.
AI.result: Parallel efficiency-aware malleability reduced malleable workload time by 27%, maintained resource utilization, but introduced queueing delays for individual jobs.
AI.conclusion: The methodology effectively validates DRM techniques like malleability, demonstrating their practicality and benefits in real-world HPC conditions while balancing efficiency and system utilization.

title: Persuadability and LLMs as Legal Decision Tools
comment: Accepted to 21st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Law (ICAIL 2026)
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are proposed as legal decision assistants, and even first-instance decision-makers, across a range of judicial and administrative contexts, it becomes essential to explore how they answer legal questions, and in particular the factors that lead them to decide difficult questions in one way or another. A specific feature of legal decisions is the need to respond to arguments advanced by contending parties. A legal decision-maker must be able to engage with, and respond to, including through being potentially persuaded by, arguments advanced by the parties. Conversely, they should not be unduly persuadable, influenced by a particularly compelling advocate to decide cases based on the skills of the advocates, rather than the merits of the case. We explore how frontier open- and closed-weights LLMs respond to legal arguments, reporting original experimental results examining how the quality of the advocate making those arguments affects the likelihood that a model will agree with a particular legal point of view, and exploring the factors driving these results. Our results have implications for the feasibility of adopting LLMs across legal and administrative settings.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how different Large Language Models (LLMs) engage with and respond to legal arguments, focusing on their susceptibility to be influenced by the advocate's skills versus the merit of the legal case.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the capabilities and limitations of LLMs as legal decision-assistants or decision-makers, particularly regarding their ability to objectively evaluate legal arguments.
AI.method: The authors perform experiments with various frontier LLMs (both open- and closed-weights) to investigate how the quality of advocacy affects these models' decisions and explore the factors influencing the persuasion.
AI.result: The study finds that the quality of argumentation by advocates significantly impacts LLMs' agreement with specific legal viewpoints. The factors behind these outcomes are also analyzed.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest challenges in using LLMs in legal or administrative decision-making contexts due to their susceptibility to advocacy quality, raising questions about their objectivity and reliability.

title: LLM-Assisted Empirical Software Engineering: Systematic Literature Review and Research Agenda
summary:
Context: Empirical Software Engineering (ESE) faces increasing challenges due to data scale, methodological complexity, and reproducibility concerns. Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as promising tools to support empirical workflows, yet their use remains fragmented, with no comprehensive synthesis to guide responsible adoption.
  Aims: This study analyzes how LLMs are used in ESE, examining supported tasks, phases of the empirical lifecycle, integration into workflows, reported benefits and limitations, and the extent of reproducibility-related reporting. It also identifies gaps and future research directions.
  Method: We conducted a systematic literature review of peer-reviewed papers (2020-2025) across 12 leading software engineering venues, resulting in 50 primary studies analyzed through qualitative and quantitative synthesis.
  Results: We identified 69 LLM-assisted tasks, mainly in mining software repositories and controlled experiments, focusing on classification, filtering, and evaluation. LLMs are used across multiple phases but are concentrated in data processing and analysis. Their integration is largely automation-oriented, with limited decision-support use. Benefits emphasize efficiency and scalability, while limitations include hallucinations, inconsistency, prompt sensitivity, and reproducibility issues. Reporting practices are often incomplete.
  Conclusion: LLM use in ESE is growing but remains automation-driven, with gaps in human-centered integration and transparency. We outline implications and research agenda for responsible use.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the use of large language models (LLMs) in Empirical Software Engineering (ESE), identifying their applications, benefits, limitations, and gaps in reproducibility and transparency.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the fragmented use of LLMs in ESE and provide a comprehensive synthesis to guide their responsible integration.
AI.method: A systematic literature review of 50 peer-reviewed papers from 2020-2025 across 12 software engineering venues was conducted, using both qualitative and quantitative synthesis.
AI.result: 69 tasks involving LLMs were identified, primarily in data processing and analysis. While LLMs offer efficiency and scalability, issues like hallucination, inconsistency, prompt sensitivity, and poor reporting practices were common.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are increasingly used in ESE but are mainly automation-focused. The study highlights the need for improvements in human-centered integration, reproducibility, and transparency, and suggests a research agenda for responsible LLM usage.

title: 2D and 3D Grasp Planners for the GET Asymmetrical Gripper
summary: In this paper, we introduce GET-2D-1.0, a fast grasp planner for the GET asymmetrical gripper that operates from a single-view RGB-D image, using the Ferrari-Canny metric and a novel sampling strategy, and GET-3D-1.0, a mesh-based method using a 3D gripper model and ray-tracing. We evaluate both grasp planners against baselines with physical experiments, which suggest that GET-2D-1.0 can improve over a bounding box baseline by over 40% in lift success, shake survival, and force resistance. Experiments with GET-3D-1.0 suggest slight improvement compared to GET-2D-1.0 on lift success and shake survival, but are more computationally expensive, averaging 17 seconds of planning compared to 683 ms for GET-2D-1.0.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces two grasp planners, GET-2D-1.0 and GET-3D-1.0, with evaluations showing significant performance improvements for the former and slightly better results for the latter, albeit with higher computational cost.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency and accuracy of robotic grasp planning using an asymmetrical gripper, addressing challenges in performance and computational time.
AI.method: Developed GET-2D-1.0, a fast planner utilizing a single-view RGB-D image with the Ferrari-Canny metric and novel sampling strategy, and GET-3D-1.0, a mesh-based planner using a 3D gripper model and ray-tracing. Both were experimentally evaluated.
AI.result: GET-2D-1.0 achieved a 40% improvement in lift success, shake survival, and force resistance compared to a baseline. GET-3D-1.0 showed slight improvement over GET-2D-1.0 in lift success and shake survival but with significantly higher computational cost.
AI.conclusion: GET-2D-1.0 offers a practical trade-off between performance and efficiency, while GET-3D-1.0 provides marginally better results at the expense of computational speed.

title: SpecTr-GBV: Multi-Draft Block Verification Accelerating Speculative Decoding
summary: Autoregressive language models suffer from high inference latency due to their sequential decoding nature. Speculative decoding (SD) mitigates this by employing a lightweight draft model to propose candidate tokens, which are selectively verified by a larger target model. While existing methods either adopt multi-draft strategies to increase acceptance rates or block verification techniques to jointly verify multiple tokens, they remain limited by treating these improvements in isolation. In this work, we propose SpecTr-GBV, a novel SD method that unifies multi-draft and greedy block verification (GBV) into a single framework. By formulating the verification step as an optimal transport problem over draft and target token blocks, SpecTr-GBV improves both theoretical efficiency and empirical performance. We theoretically prove that SpecTr-GBV achieves the optimal expected acceptance length physically attainable within the framework of i.i.d. draft generation, and this bound improves as the number of drafts increases. Empirically, we evaluate SpecTr-GBV across five datasets and four baselines. Our method achieves superior speedup and significantly higher block efficiency while preserving output quality. In addition, we perform comprehensive ablation studies to evaluate the impact of various hyperparameters in the model.

AI.tldr: The study introduces SpecTr-GBV, an innovative method to accelerate autoregressive language model decoding by combining multi-draft and greedy block verification (GBV) techniques for higher efficiency.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language models encounter high latency in sequential decoding, necessitating faster and more efficient decoding mechanisms.
AI.method: SpecTr-GBV merges multi-draft strategies with greedy block verification (GBV), framing the verification step as an optimal transport problem over token blocks.
AI.result: Experiments across five datasets and four baselines demonstrate that SpecTr-GBV offers faster decoding with improved block efficiency, maintaining high-quality output.
AI.conclusion: SpecTr-GBV efficiently bridges theoretical and empirical aspects to enhance computational efficiency in language models, supported by ablation studies.

title: Laplace Approximation for Bayesian Tensor Network Kernel Machines
comment: 19 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables. Code available at: https://github.com/AlbMLpy/laplace-tnkm
summary: Uncertainty estimation is essential for robust decision-making in the presence of ambiguous or out-of-distribution inputs. Gaussian Processes (GPs) are classical kernel-based models that offer principled uncertainty quantification and perform well on small- to medium-scale datasets. Alternatively, formulating the weight space learning problem under tensor network assumptions yields scalable tensor network kernel machines. However, these assumptions break Gaussianity, complicating standard probabilistic inference. This raises a fundamental question: how can tensor network kernel machines provide principled uncertainty estimates? We propose a novel Bayesian Tensor Network Kernel Machine (LA-TNKM) that employs a (linearized) Laplace approximation for Bayesian inference. A comprehensive set of numerical experiments shows that the proposed method consistently matches or surpasses Gaussian Processes and Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) across diverse UCI regression benchmarks, highlighting both its effectiveness and practical relevance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LA-TNKM, a new method using Bayesian inference for uncertainty estimation, outperforming GPs and BNNs in regression benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of providing principled uncertainty estimates for tensor network kernel machines, which lose Gaussianity and standard probabilistic inference capability.
AI.method: Proposed a Bayesian Tensor Network Kernel Machine (LA-TNKM) that uses a linearized Laplace approximation for Bayesian inference.
AI.result: LA-TNKM matches or outperforms Gaussian Processes and Bayesian Neural Networks in various UCI regression benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: LA-TNKM offers a scalable and effective approach for uncertainty estimation, with performance benefits in regression tasks, showcasing its practical utility.

title: Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management
summary: Frontier AI both amplifies existing risks and introduces qualitatively novel challenges. Not only is there a notable lack of stable scientific consensus resulting from the rapid pace of technological change, but emerging frontier AI safety practices are often misaligned with, or may undermine, established risk management frameworks. To address these challenges, we systematically surface open problems in frontier AI risk management. Adopting a problem-oriented approach, we examine each stage of the risk management process - risk planning, identification, analysis, evaluation, and mitigation - through a structured review of the literature, identifying unresolved challenges and the actors best positioned to address them. Recognising that different types of open problems call for different responses, we classify open problems according to whether they reflect (a) a lack of scientific or technical consensus, (b) misalignment with, or challenges to, established risk management frameworks, or (c) shortcomings in implementation despite apparent consensus and alignment. By mapping these open problems and identifying the actors best positioned to address them - including developers, deployers, regulators, standards bodies, researchers, and third-party evaluators - this work aims to clarify where progress is needed to enable robust and meaningful consensus on frontier AI risk management.The paper does not propose specific solutions; instead, it provides a problem-oriented, agenda-setting reference document, complemented by a living online repository, intended to support coordination, reduce duplication, and guide future research and governance efforts.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and categorizes unresolved challenges in managing risks associated with frontier AI technologies.
AI.motivation: To address gaps and misalignments in risk management practices due to the rapid evolution and unique risks posed by frontier AI.
AI.method: The authors systematically review literature on frontier AI risk management stages, classify open problems, and map actors responsible for addressing them.
AI.result: A structured classification of frontier AI risk issues, grouped by scientific consensus, framework alignment, and implementation shortcomings.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides guidance for improved collaboration and governance in frontier AI risk management without proposing specific solutions, but includes an online repository for ongoing updates.

title: Sample Selection Using Multi-Task Autoencoders in Federated Learning with Non-IID Data
comment: Published in Engineering Science and Technology, an International Journal, 61 (2025), 101920. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jestch.2024.101920 and Codes: https://github.com/eardic/FL_DPQS
summary: Federated learning is a machine learning paradigm in which multiple devices collaboratively train a model under the supervision of a central server while ensuring data privacy. However, its performance is often hindered by redundant, malicious, or abnormal samples, leading to model degradation and inefficiency. To overcome these issues, we propose novel sample selection methods for image classification, employing a multitask autoencoder to estimate sample contributions through loss and feature analysis. Our approach incorporates unsupervised outlier detection, using one-class support vector machine (OCSVM), isolation forest (IF), and adaptive loss threshold (AT) methods managed by a central server to filter noisy samples on clients. We also propose a multi-class deep support vector data description (SVDD) loss controlled by a central server to enhance feature-based sample selection. We validate our methods on CIFAR10 and MNIST datasets across varying numbers of clients, non-IID distributions, and noise levels up to 40%. The results show significant accuracy improvements with loss-based sample selection, achieving gains of up to 7.02% on CIFAR10 with OCSVM and 1.83% on MNIST with AT. Additionally, our federated SVDD loss further improves feature-based sample selection, yielding accuracy gains of up to 0.99% on CIFAR10 with OCSVM. These results show the effectiveness of our methods in improving model accuracy across various client counts and noise conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces sample selection methods in federated learning using multitask autoencoders and various techniques like OCSVM, IF, and AT, demonstrating significant performance improvements on CIFAR10 and MNIST datasets.
AI.motivation: Current federated learning models are negatively impacted by redundant, malicious, or abnormal samples, causing inefficiencies and degraded model accuracy.
AI.method: The study employs multitask autoencoders to estimate sample contributions based on loss and feature analysis, combined with outlier filtering using OCSVM, IF, and AT methods, as well as a federated deep SVDD loss for feature-based selection.
AI.result: The proposed methods yield substantial accuracy improvements: up to 7.02% on CIFAR10 and 1.83% on MNIST for loss-based selection, and up to 0.99% gains on feature-based selection with federated SVDD.
AI.conclusion: The novel sample-selection approaches effectively enhance federated learning model performance by mitigating the influence of noisy or abnormal samples, particularly in challenging conditions like non-IID distributions and high noise levels.

title: Recent Advances in mm-Wave and Sub-THz/THz Oscillators for FutureG Technologies
summary: This paper provides a concise yet comprehensive review of recent advancements in millimeter-wave (mm-wave) oscillators below 100 GHz and sub-terahertz (sub-THz/THz) oscillators above 100 GHz for next-generation computing and communication systems, including 5G, 6G, and beyond. Various design approaches, including CMOS, SiGe, and III-V semiconductor technologies, are explored in terms of performance metrics such as phase noise, output power, efficiency, frequency tunability, and stability. The review highlights key challenges in achieving high-performance and reliable oscillator designs while discussing emerging techniques for performance enhancement. By evaluating recent design trends, this work aims to offer valuable insights and design guidelines that facilitate the development of robust mm-wave and sub-THz/THz oscillators for future communication, computing, and sensing applications.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews millimeter-wave and sub-terahertz oscillators, detailing advancements, challenges, and emerging techniques for 5G, 6G, and future systems.
AI.motivation: To address the demand for high-performance and reliable oscillators for next-generation communication and computing systems.
AI.method: A detailed evaluation of various oscillator designs using CMOS, SiGe, and III-V technologies based on metrics like phase noise, output power, efficiency, tunability, and stability.
AI.result: The paper identifies challenges in oscillator design and highlights emerging techniques to enhance performance, providing design trends and guidelines.
AI.conclusion: It provides insights into robust mm-wave and sub-THz/THz oscillator development to facilitate future communication, computing, and sensing applications.

title: DMRlib: Easy-coding and Efficient Resource Management for Job Malleability
summary: Process malleability has proved to have a highly positive impact on the resource utilization and global productivity in data centers compared with the conventional static resource allocation policy. However, the non-negligible additional development effort this solution imposes has constrained its adoption by the scientific programming community. In this work, we present DMRlib, a library designed to offer the global advantages of process malleability while providing a minimalist MPI-like syntax. The library includes a series of predefined communication patterns that greatly ease the development of malleable applications. In addition, we deploy several scenarios to demonstrate the positive impact of process malleability featuring different scalability patterns. Concretely, we study two job submission modes (rigid and moldable) in order to identify the best-case scenarios for malleability using metrics such as resource allocation rate, completed jobs per second, and energy consumption. The experiments prove that our elastic approach may improve global throughput by a factor higher than 3x compared to the traditional workloads of non-malleable jobs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DMRlib, a library simplifying process malleability for data centers. It improves throughput over 3x compared to non-malleable approaches.
AI.motivation: Process malleability significantly improves resource utilization and productivity in data centers, but its adoption is hindered by the additional development effort it requires.
AI.method: The authors developed DMRlib, a library with MPI-like syntax and predefined communication patterns to streamline the development of malleable applications. They also tested malleability in job scheduling with metrics like allocation rate and energy use.
AI.result: The experiments demonstrated substantial improvements in global throughput (over 3x) using their elastic approach compared to static resource allocation.
AI.conclusion: DMRlib successfully combines simplicity and the efficiency of process malleability, demonstrating its potential to enhance productivity and resource utilization in scalable systems.

title: Apriori-based Analysis of Learned Helplessness in Mathematics Tutoring: Behavioral Patterns by Level, Intervention, and Outcome
summary: This study applied the Apriori algorithm to analyze behavioral interaction patterns associated with learned helplessness (LH) in mathematics tutoring system logs. Interaction data were examined across three dimensions: LH level (low vs. high), system-based intervention (with vs. without), and problem-solving outcomes (solved vs. unsolved). The analysis of the complete dataset showed that skipping problems without using hints was the most frequent pattern linked to unsolved outcomes, while persistence behaviors such as not skipping were less dominant overall. Comparisons by LH level showed that low-LH students had stronger links between problem solving and not skipping, as well as positive associations between hint use and solved outcomes. High-LH students showed more avoidance patterns, with skipping strongly tied to unsolved outcomes. In the comparison of system-based intervention conditions, students without intervention had the highest lift for persistence-success links, while the with-intervention group had stronger patterns involving skipping behaviors leading to unsolved outcomes. Outcome-specific analysis showed that not skipping was consistently associated with solved problems across all groups, while skipping without hints predicted unsolved outcomes. Practical implications and recommendations are discussed.

AI.tldr: The study used the Apriori algorithm to analyze behaviors linked to learned helplessness in math tutoring systems, focusing on skipping problems and persistence patterns.
AI.motivation: Understanding how behavioral patterns relate to learned helplessness and problem-solving success in tutoring systems.
AI.method: Apriori algorithm was applied across three dimensions: LH level, system-based intervention, and problem-solving outcomes.
AI.result: Low-LH students linked not skipping and solved outcomes; High-LH students showed avoidance like skipping tied to unsolved outcomes. Interventions affected persistence and skipping patterns.
AI.conclusion: Persistence behaviors (like not skipping) lead to success, while skipping without hints predicts failure. These findings provide actionable suggestions for improving tutoring interventions.

title: Agentic AI in the Software Development Lifecycle: Architecture, Empirical Evidence, and the Reshaping of Software Engineering
summary: The arrival of large language models (LLMs) capable of multi-step reasoning, tool use, and long-horizon planning has produced a qualitative shift in software engineering. Where earlier code-completion tools such as GitHub Copilot operated at the granularity of a line or function, modern agentic systems -- Claude Code, OpenAI Codex CLI, Google Jules, Devin, OpenHands, SWE-agent, MetaGPT, ChatDev, and DeepMind's AlphaEvolve -- operate at the granularity of a repository, a feature, or an algorithm. We synthesize work from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft Research, Princeton, Stanford, and the broader academic community to characterize this transition. We propose a six-layer reference architecture for agentic software engineering systems, contrast a traditional Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) with an emerging Agentic SDLC (A-SDLC), and consolidate empirical evidence on performance (a rise from 1.96% to 78.4% on SWE-bench Verified between October 2023 and April 2026), productivity (13.6%-55.8% time savings across controlled studies), and labor-market impact (49% of jobs sampled by Anthropic in 2026 saw AI used for at least a quarter of their tasks). We argue that the central object of inquiry has shifted from code generation to delegated execution under human supervision, and we identify five open problems -- evaluation, governance, technical debt, skill redistribution, and the economics of attention -- that will determine whether the agentic transition is net-positive for the discipline.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how large language models have revolutionized software engineering by introducing agentic systems that work at higher granularities (e.g., repositories or features). It presents a six-layer architecture for such systems, compares traditional and agentic software lifecycles, and discusses performance improvements, productivity gains, and labor-market impacts.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore the transformative effect of advanced large language models on software engineering, as they enable complex, long-horizon tasks and shift the focus from simple code generation to delegated execution with human oversight.
AI.method: The paper synthesizes research from multiple leading institutions to develop a six-layer reference architecture for agentic systems, contrasts traditional software lifecycles with agentic ones, and analyzes empirical data on performance, productivity, and labor impact.
AI.result: The findings show significant performance improvements (up to 78.4% on a benchmark), productivity gains (up to 55.8% time savings), and labor market changes (AI tools used in 49% of jobs, for ≥25% of tasks) by 2026.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that software engineering is shifting towards agentic systems, driven by delegated execution under human supervision. However, it highlights that open issues like evaluation, governance, and skill redistribution will be critical in ensuring this transformation is beneficial.

title: Split over $n$ resource sharing problem: Are fewer capable agents better than many simpler ones?
comment: Short paper presented at the 15th International Conference on Swarm Intelligence (ANTS 2026)
summary: In multi-agent systems, should limited resources be concentrated into a few capable agents or distributed among many simpler ones? This work formulates the split over $n$ resource sharing problem where a group of $n$ agents equally shares a common resource (e.g., monetary budget, computational resources, physical size). We present a case study in multi-agent coverage where the area of the disk-shaped footprint of agents scales as $1/n$. A formal analysis reveals that the initial coverage rate grows with $n$. However, if the speed of agents decreases proportionally with their radii, groups of all sizes perform equally well, whereas if it decreases proportionally with their footprints, a single agent performs best. We also present computer simulations in which resource splitting increases the failure rates of individual agents. The models and findings help identify optimal distributiveness levels and inform the design of multi-agent systems under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: The study investigates whether resources should be centralized with fewer agents or distributed to many simpler ones in multi-agent systems. Results show varying outcomes based on agents' performance metrics.
AI.motivation: To determine the optimal way to allocate limited resources — centralized or distributed — in multi-agent systems, and to understand the effects of such allocation strategies on performance.
AI.method: The paper formulates a resource-sharing problem, conducts formal analysis on agent coverage, derives scaling relationships based on resource distribution, and incorporates computer simulations to analyze failure rates.
AI.result: Initial coverage rate increases with the number of agents, but performance level and resource split depend on speed scaling. Resource splitting can increase individual agent failure rates.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the trade-offs between centralization and distribution, aiding in designing efficient multi-agent systems by identifying the best resource allocation strategy under constraints.

title: MATH-PT: A Math Reasoning Benchmark for European and Brazilian Portuguese
comment: Accepted at 17th International Conference on Computational Processing of Portuguese (PROPOR 2026). Open access to dataset repo https://huggingface.co/datasets/tiagoteixeira03/MATH-PT and model outputs https://github.com/deep-spin/math-benchmark
summary: The use of large language models (LLMs) for complex mathematical reasoning is an emergent area of research, with fast progress in methods, models, and benchmark datasets. However, most mathematical reasoning evaluations exhibit a significant linguistic bias, with the vast majority of benchmark datasets being exclusively in English or (at best) translated from English. We address this limitation by introducing {\sc Math-PT}, a novel dataset comprising 1,729 mathematical problems written in European and Brazilian Portuguese. {\sc Math-PT} is curated from a variety of high-quality native sources, including mathematical Olympiads, competitions, and exams from Portugal and Brazil. We present a comprehensive benchmark of current state-of-the-art LLMs on {\sc Math-PT}, revealing that frontier reasoning models achieve strong performance in multiple choice questions compared to open weight models, but that their performance decreases for questions with figures or open-ended questions. To facilitate future research, we release the benchmark dataset and model outputs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Math-PT, a Portuguese mathematical reasoning dataset, addresses linguistic bias in current benchmarks, and evaluates LLMs on it.
